content.full_text:
Farn Polrrtcal PnecrrcEs CouprrssroN
428 J Street r Suite 620 o Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 . Fax (916) 322-0886
November 4,2003
William H. Wainwright
Martinez City Council
1153 Hillside Drive
Martinez, CA94553
Re:
Your Request for Advice
Our File No. 4.-03-235
Dear Mr. Wainwright:
This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").I The issue concerns a
decision scheduled for the November 5,2003, city council meeting.
QUESTION
Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from voting on
Mr. Keller's application for the disposition of city-owned real property eind the merger
and combination of other parcels located within 500 feet of your residential real property?
CONCLUSION
It is presumed that this decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interest in your residential real property and that you will have a conflict of
interest in this decision. However, as you were advised in the WainwrightLetter No. A-
03-179, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have any frnancial effect on your property.
Reasonable reliance on an appraisal by a disinterested and otherwise qualified real estate
professional, if based on an accurate understanding of the pertinent facts and
circumstances, including the factors listed in regulation 18705.2 (bxlXA)-(C), will
generally be sufficient to rebut this presumption.
I Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at Title 2, sections
18109-18997, of the Califomia Code of Regulations.


File No. A-03-235
Page No. 2
FACTS
You are a mernber of the Martinez City Council. You own residential property
that is within 500 feet of residential property owned by Mr. Keller, who has a pending
application with the City of Martinezto transfer a portion of a city-owned parcel
adjoining his property and to combine and merge parcels ofproperty.
You are presumed to have a conflict of interest preventing you from participating
in the council's decision on this matter, due to the proximity of your residence to Mr.
Keller's residence and the other subject properties ("Keller property''). Therefore, you
offer an appraisal from Mr. Michael Herwood, dated October 7,2003. Mr. Herwood's
appraisal analyzes the subject property, which includes Mr. Keller's residence on Thomas
Drive, the abandonment of "paper street," a portion of Park Street, and the merger of
parcels 372-153-034 and 035, and the combing of parcels 372-111-024 and372-152-OOl.
Mr. Keller is applying to the city for the abandonment of a street easement and to merge
and combine some parcels with his residential property.
The appraiser states that he has used the criteria specified in regulation
18705.2@)(1)(A)-(C) to determine whether your voting on this matter "could reasonably
have any financial effect" on the value of your residence. Mr. Herwood indicates that in
his opinion,"aty boundary adjustment, street abandonment or sale will have no financial
impact whatsoever on your property or on the properties bordering yours, not even one
penny." He further states that his opinion is "limited to the impact of the boundary
adjustment, lot merger or street abandonment on your property, and properties bordering
yours."
ANALYSIS
The primary pu{pose of the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions is to ensure that
"[p]ublic officials, whether elected or appointed, [should] perform their duties in an
impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the financial
interests of persons who have supported them." (Section 81001(b).) In furtherance of
this goal, section 87100 prohibits a public official from making, participating in making,
or otherwise using his or her official position to influence a govemmental decision in
which the public official has a financial interest.
In order to determine the existence of a conflict of interest, regulation 18700
provides an eight-step analysis, as discussed below:


File No. A-03-235
Page No. 3
Questions One and Two: Are you considered a "public officialr,, and are you
making, participating in making, or influencing a governmental decision?
As a member of the Martinez City Council, you are a member of a state or local
govenrment agency and, therefore, you are a public official subject to the conflict-of-
interest provisions of the Act. (Section 82048; regulation 13701(a).)
A public official "makes a goveflrmental decision" when the official, acting
within the authority of his or her office or position, votes on a matter, obligates or
commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreernent on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18702.7.) As a member of the
city council, you will make a govemmental decision if you vote on the city council
decision regarding Mr. Keller's application and the Keller property.
Question Three: What is your economic interest - 
the possible source of a conflict
of interest?
Section 87103 provides that a public official has a o'financial interest" in a
govemmental decision "if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public generally, on the
official, a member of his or her immediate family," or on any of the official's economic
interests, as described in section 87103. As pertains to the pending decision, your
economic interest is your real property:
A public official has an economic interest in real property in which he or she has a
direct or indirect interest of $2,000 or more (section S7103(b); regulation
18703.2);
Since you have not inquired about any other economic interest potentially
affected by this decision, we limit our analysis to the potential effect of this decision on
your residential real property interest.
Question Four: Is your economic interest directly or indirectly involved in the
governmental decision?
Real property is directly involved in a governmental decision if that real property
is located within 500 feet of the boundaries (or proposed boundaries) of the property
which is the subject of the governmental decision. (Regulation 18704.2(aXl).) Your
residential real property is therefore directly involved in the city council decision
regarding Mr. Keller's application and the Keller property.


File No. A-03-235
Page No. 4
Questions Five and Six: What is the size of a financial effect on your real property
that would be considered 66materialr" and is it reasonably foreseeabte that the
financial effect on your economic interest will be material?
Any frnancial effect of a governmental decision on real property directly involved
in the governmental decision is presumed to be material. @egulation 18705.2(axl).)
This presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable that the
governmental decision will have any financial effect on the real property. (Ibid.) The
size of the financial effect does not matter; if there is any financial effect at all, that effect
is presumed to be "material."
An effect on an economic interest is considered "reasonably foreseeable" if there
is a substantial likelihood that it will occur. (Regulation 18706(a).) A financial effect
need not be certain to be considered reasonably foreseeable, but it must be more than a
mere possibility. (In re Thorner (1975) I FPPC Ops. 198.)
Whether a material financial effect is or is not reasonably foreseeable is
necessarily afactttal question that the public official is ultimately responsible to decide.
You have provided us a copy of Mr. Herwood's October 7,2003, appraisal. As a
qualified real estate appraiser, he offers his opinion on the financial effect of the city
council decision regarding the Keller application/property on your residential real
property. Among other things, Mr. Herwood stated that he considered the factors
specifi ed in regulatio n I 87 05 .2(bX I XA)-(C), and concluded that "any boundary
adjustment, street abandonment or sale will have no financial impact whatsoever on yogr
property or the properties bordering yours, not even one penny."
An appraisalby a disinterested and otherwise qualified real estate professional,
based on an accurate understanding of all pertinent facts and circumstances, including
those listed as factors in regulation 18705.2(bXlXA)-(C), will generally be considered a
good faith effort by a public official to assess the financial effect of a decision on his or
her real property, suflicient to rebut the presumption of regulation 18705.2(a)(l).
(Wainwrtghr Advice Letter, A-03-179; Wallace Advice Letter, No. 4-03-069; Yadon
Advice Letter, No. A-02-080.) Of course, the presumption can be rebutted only when the
appraisal concludes that there will be no financial effect on the official's real property, as
the appraiser has concluded in your case.
However, a public official may not simply rely on a third-party appraisal without
further inquiry into the qualifications of the appraiser, whether he or she considered the
factors listed in our regulations, and whether the conclusions reached by the appraiser are
objectively defensible, that is, based on a full and accurate assessment of all pertinant
facts and circumstances. When a third-party appraisal meets these standards, as it
appears to do in this case, a public official may rely on the appraisal to conclude that the
presumption of regulation 18705.2(a)(l) has been rebutted.2
'Please bear in mind the important qualification that the Commission does not act as a finder of
fact in its advice giving capacity, and that ultimate responsibility for the reasonable assessment and
discloswe of all pertinent facts rests with the public official. (See, e.g. Oderman Advice Letter, No. A-02-
340; O'Harra Advice Letter, A-00-174.)


File No. A-03-235
Page No. 5
Questions Seven and Eight: "Public Generally" and c'Legally Required
Participation" Exceptions
Because it seems that you have properly rebutted the presumption of regulation
18705.2(a)(1), and because you offer no information suggesting that an exception to the
Act's conflict-of-interest provisions might otherwise be applicable to the proposed city
council decision, we end our analysis without considering the possible application of
these two exceptions to the conflict-of-interest rules.
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Luisa Menchaca
General Counsel
By:
S
Legal Division
JS je

QUESTION: Do the Act's conflict-of-interest provisions prohibit you from voting on
Mr. Keller's application for the disposition of city-owned real property eind the merger
and combination of other parcels located within 500 feet of your residential real property?

CONCLUSION: It is presumed that this decision will have a material financial effect on your
economic interest in your residential real property and that you will have a conflict of
interest in this decision. However, as you were advised in the WainwrightLetter No. A-
03-179, this presumption may be rebutted by proof that it is not reasonably foreseeable
that the governmental decision will have any frnancial effect on your property.
Reasonable reliance on an appraisal by a disinterested and otherwise qualified real estate
professional, if based on an accurate understanding of the pertinent facts and
circumstances, including the factors listed in regulation 18705.2 (bxlXA)-(C), will
generally be sufficient to rebut this presumption.
